category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Similar Questions/Part B/Real Analysis — Uniform Convergence of Function Sequences

questiontext.text: The sequence \(f_n(x)=x^n\) on \([0,1]\) converges:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Pointwise but NOT uniformly to \(f(x)=0\) for \(x\in[0,1)\) and \(f(1)=1\)

For \(x\in[0,1)\): \(x^n\to0\). For \(x=1\): \(1^n=1\to1\). So pointwise limit is discontinuous. Since uniform convergence of continuous functions yields a continuous limit, and the limit is discontinuous, convergence is NOT uniform on \([0,1]\). It is uniform on \([0,r]\) for any \(r<1\).

answer.text: Uniformly on every closed subinterval of \([0,1]\)

questiontext.text: Let \(g_n(x)=\dfrac{nx}{1+n^2x^2}\) on \(\mathbb{R}\). The pointwise limit as \(n\to\infty\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(g(x)=0\) for all \(x\)

For \(x=0\): \(g_n(0)=0\). For \(x\neq0\): \(g_n(x)=\frac{x/n}{1/n^2+x^2}\to\frac{0}{x^2}=0\). So \(g\equiv0\). But \(g_n(1/n)=\frac{1/n\cdot n}{1+1}=1/2\not\to0\), so convergence is NOT uniform on \(\mathbb{R}\). On \([\delta,\infty)\) for \(\delta>0\) it is uniform.

questiontext.text: A sequence \(\{f_n\}\) converges uniformly on \(E\) if and only if (uniform Cauchy criterion):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) For every \(\epsilon>0\), there exists \(N\) such that \(m,n>N\Rightarrow\sup_{x\in E}|f_m(x)-f_n(x)|<\epsilon\)

This is the uniform Cauchy criterion: equivalent to uniform convergence in complete spaces. The sup-norm \(\|f_m-f_n\|_\infty=\sup_E|f_m-f_n|\to0\) is the correct measure of uniform closeness.

answer.text: For every \(x\in E\) and \(\epsilon>0\), there exists \(N(x)\) such that \(n>N(x)\Rightarrow|f_n(x)-f(x)|<\epsilon\)
For every \(\epsilon>0\), \(\exists N\) such that \(m,n>N\Rightarrow\sup_{x\in E}|f_m(x)-f_n(x)|<\epsilon\)

name.text: Uniform Convergence - Limit of Continuous Functions

questiontext.text: If \(f_n\to f\) uniformly on \([a,b]\) and each \(f_n\) is continuous, then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(f\) is continuous on \([a,b]\)

Uniform limit of continuous functions is continuous. Proof: \(|f(x)-f(y)|\leq|f(x)-f_n(x)|+|f_n(x)-f_n(y)|+|f_n(y)-f(y)|<\epsilon/3+\epsilon/3+\epsilon/3=\epsilon\). The first and third terms are controlled by uniform convergence; the middle by continuity of \(f_n\).

name.text: Uniform Convergence - Interchange of Limit and Integral

questiontext.text: If \(f_n\to f\) uniformly on \([a,b]\) and each \(f_n\) is Riemann integrable, then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\displaystyle\int_a^b f_n\to\int_a^b f\)

Uniform convergence allows interchange of limit and integral: \(|\int f_n-\int f|\leq\int|f_n-f|\leq(b-a)\sup|f_n-f|\to0\). This is the key theorem justifying term-by-term integration of uniformly convergent series.

questiontext.text: The sequence \(f_n(x)=\dfrac{\sin(nx)}{n}\) on \(\mathbb{R}\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Converges uniformly to 0 on \(\mathbb{R}\)

\(\sup_x|f_n(x)-0|=\sup_x|\sin(nx)/n|\leq1/n\to0\). So the convergence is uniform. However, \(f_n'(x)=\cos(nx)\) does NOT converge (oscillates), showing that uniform convergence of \(f_n\) does not imply convergence of \(f_n'\).

answer.text: Converges pointwise but NOT uniformly to 0

questiontext.text: If \(f_n\to f\) uniformly on \([a,b]\), then the derivatives satisfy \(f_n'\to f'\) provided:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) The derivatives \(f_n'\) converge uniformly on \([a,b]\)

The correct theorem: if \(f_n\to f\) pointwise (or at one point), and \(f_n'\) converges uniformly, then \(f'=\lim f_n'\). Uniform convergence of \(f_n\) alone is insufficient for differentiating limits. Example: \(f_n(x)=\sin(nx)/\sqrt{n}\to0\) uniformly but \(f_n'(x)=\sqrt{n}\cos(nx)\) diverges.

questiontext.text: The Weierstrass M-test guarantees uniform convergence of \(\sum f_n\) on \(E\) if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(|f_n(x)|\leq M_n\) for all \(x\in E\) and \(\sum M_n<\infty\)

Weierstrass M-test: dominating by a convergent series of constants implies absolute and uniform convergence. This is the most commonly applied test for uniform convergence of series, e.g., proving a power series converges uniformly inside its radius of convergence.

answer.text: \(|f_n(x)|\leq M_n\) for all \(x\in E\) and \(\sum M_n<\infty\)
The pointwise limit exists and is bounded

questiontext.text: Let \(f_n(x)=n^2 x e^{-nx}\) on \([0,\infty)\). The pointwise limit is \(f\equiv0\). Is the convergence uniform on \([0,\infty)\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) No, the convergence is not uniform

\(f_n(1/n)=n^2\cdot(1/n)\cdot e^{-1}=ne^{-1}\to\infty\). So \(\sup_{x\geq0}|f_n(x)-0|\geq ne^{-1}\to\infty\neq0\). The convergence is pointwise to 0 but NOT uniform. Furthermore, \(\int_0^\infty f_n\,dx=n^2\int_0^\infty xe^{-nx}dx=n^2/n^2=1\not\to\int_0^\infty0\,dx=0\), confirming non-uniformity.

answer.text: Yes, the convergence is uniform on \([0,\infty)\)
The sequence does not converge pointwise

questiontext.text: Dini's theorem gives uniform convergence when \(f_n\searrow f\) (monotone decreasing) on compact \(K\), each \(f_n\) continuous, and:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) The limit function \(f\) is continuous

Dini's theorem: monotone convergence of continuous functions to a continuous limit on a compact set is uniform. The four conditions are: (1) \(K\) compact, (2) each \(f_n\) continuous, (3) \(f_n\searrow f\) (or \(\nearrow\)), (4) \(f\) continuous. All four are needed — dropping any one makes it fail.

questiontext.text: \(f_n\to f\) uniformly on \(E\) if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\|f_n-f\|_\infty=\sup_{x\in E}|f_n(x)-f(x)|\to0\)

Uniform convergence is exactly convergence in the sup-norm. This makes \(C(K)\) (continuous functions on compact \(K\)) a complete metric space — a Banach space. The sup-norm is the correct measure of uniform approximation.

name.text: Uniform Convergence - Power Series Inside Radius

questiontext.text: A power series \(\sum_{n=0}^\infty a_n x^n\) with radius of convergence \(R>0\) converges uniformly on:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Every closed interval \([-r,r]\) with \(0

A power series converges absolutely for \(|x|

questiontext.text: Arzelà–Ascoli theorem: a sequence \(\{f_n\}\) of continuous functions on \([a,b]\) has a uniformly convergent subsequence if the family is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Uniformly bounded and equicontinuous

Arzelà–Ascoli: a subset of \(C([a,b])\) is relatively compact (every sequence has a convergent subsequence) iff it is uniformly bounded and equicontinuous. Equicontinuity: \(\forall\epsilon>0,\exists\delta>0\) such that \(|x-y|<\delta\Rightarrow|f_n(x)-f_n(y)|<\epsilon\) for ALL \(n\).

questiontext.text: Let \(f_n(x)=\dfrac{1}{1+nx}\) on \([0,1]\). The pointwise limit \(f\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) \(f(0)=1,\;f(x)=0\) for \(x\in(0,1]\); convergence is NOT uniform on \([0,1]\)

At \(x=0\): \(f_n(0)=1\to1\). At \(x>0\): \(1/(1+nx)\to0\). The limit is discontinuous at 0. Uniform limit of continuous functions must be continuous, so convergence is NOT uniform. It IS uniform on \([\delta,1]\) for any \(\delta>0\).

answer.text: \(f(0)=1,\;f(x)=0\) for \(x>0\); convergence is NOT uniform on \([0,1]\)

questiontext.text: Which of the following CANNOT be concluded from pointwise convergence \(f_n\to f\) alone?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\int_a^b f_n = \int_a^b f\)

Pointwise convergence alone does NOT guarantee interchange of limit and integral. Example: \(f_n=n\mathbf{1}_{[0,1/n]}\to0\) pointwise but \(\int_0^1f_n=1\neq0=\int_0^1f\). Uniform convergence is sufficient (but not necessary) for this interchange.

answer.text: Pointwise limit exists at each point
\(f\) is measurable if each \(f_n\) is measurable

questiontext.text: Let \(f_n(x)=\dfrac{x}{1+nx^2}\) on \(\mathbb{R}\). Then \(\|f_n\|_\infty = \sup_x|f_n(x)|\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{n}}\)

Maximise \(f_n(x)=x/(1+nx^2)\): set \(f_n'(x)=0\Rightarrow(1+nx^2)-x\cdot2nx=0\Rightarrow1-nx^2=0\Rightarrow x=1/\sqrt{n}\). Then \(f_n(1/\sqrt{n})=(1/\sqrt{n})/(1+1)=1/(2\sqrt{n})\to0\). So \(\|f_n\|_\infty=1/(2\sqrt{n})\to0\), confirming uniform convergence to 0 on \(\mathbb{R}\).

questiontext.text: The series \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{\cos(nx)}{n^2}\) converges uniformly on \(\mathbb{R}\) because:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Weierstrass M-test with \(M_n=1/n^2\) and \(\sum 1/n^2<\infty\)

\(|\cos(nx)/n^2|\leq1/n^2\) for all \(x\). Since \(\sum1/n^2=\pi^2/6<\infty\), the M-test gives absolute and uniform convergence on \(\mathbb{R}\). The sum is a continuous function of \(x\) (uniform limit of continuous functions).

answer.text: Each partial sum is bounded by 1

questiontext.text: For \(f_n(x)=x^n\), convergence is uniform on \([0,r]\) for any fixed \(r<1\), but not on \([0,1]\). The key difference is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \([0,r]\) does not contain 1, so \(\sup_{[0,r]}x^n=r^n\to0\); but \(\sup_{[0,1]}x^n=1\not\to0\)

On \([0,r]\): \(\|f_n\|_\infty=r^n\to0\) since \(r<1\). On \([0,1]\): \(\sup|f_n|=1^n=1\not\to0\). The boundary point \(x=1\) causes the failure of uniform convergence — a phenomenon called "boundary layer" in analysis.

answer.text: The functions are not continuous at \(r\)

questiontext.text: Uniform convergence \(f_n\rightrightarrows f\) on \([a,b]\) implies \(L^2\) convergence \(\|f_n-f\|_2\to0\). Is the converse true?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) No; \(L^2\) convergence does not imply uniform convergence

Uniform \(\Rightarrow L^2\): \(\|f_n-f\|_2^2=\int|f_n-f|^2\leq(b-a)\|f_n-f\|_\infty^2\to0\). But \(L^2\not\Rightarrow\) uniform: the "sliding bump" \(f_n=\mathbf{1}_{[j/k,(j+1)/k]}\) (where \(n=(k,j)\)) converges to 0 in \(L^2\) but not uniformly or even pointwise.

answer.text: No; \(L^2\) convergence does NOT imply uniform convergence
Yes, if the functions are also monotone
Yes, if the functions are also equicontinuous

questiontext.text: Let \(f_n(x)=\dfrac{n^2x}{1+n^3x^2}\) on \([0,1]\). Which statement is TRUE?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(f_n\to0\) pointwise on \([0,1]\) but the convergence is not uniform

At \(x=0\): \(f_n(0)=0\). At \(x>0\): \(n^2x/(1+n^3x^2)\sim n^2x/(n^3x^2)=1/(nx)\to0\). So pointwise limit is 0. But \(f_n(1/n)=n^2/n\cdot(1/(1+n^3/n^2))=n/(1+n)\to1\neq0\). So convergence is not uniform.
